record.field: ToolJet is an open-source low-code platform for building and deploying internal tools, dashboards, and business applications fast.

ToolJet is an open-source low-code platform for building and deploying internal tools, dashboards, and business applications fast.

It pairs a drag-and-drop visual builder with the ability to connect to dozens of databases, APIs, cloud storage services, and SaaS tools, so teams can assemble production-grade internal apps without writing a full frontend and backend. JavaScript and Python can be embedded where custom logic is needed, and a built-in PostgreSQL-backed database and workflow engine cover data storage and automation. It targets developers, IT teams, and product managers who need to ship internal software quickly while retaining control over data and security.

Key features
  • Visual app builder with dozens of responsive UI components (tables, charts, forms, lists)
  • 80+ connectors for databases, REST/GraphQL APIs, cloud storage, and SaaS platforms
  • Built-in no-code database and visual query builder backed by PostgreSQL
  • Event-driven workflow automation with conditions, loops, and multi-step orchestration
  • Embedded JavaScript and Python for custom logic and transformations
  • Enterprise security: SSO/SAML/OIDC/LDAP, RBAC, 2FA, encryption, and audit logs
Best for

Engineering and IT teams building internal admin panels, back-office tools, CRUD apps, support consoles, and operational dashboards who want self-hosting, granular access control, and the freedom to extend with code.

field.name: tag_ids
field.eval: [Command.set([ref('agz_infra_suite.tag_self_hosted'), ref('agz_infra_suite.tag_dev_tools'), ref('agz_infra_suite.tag_no_code'), ref('agz_infra_suite.tag_automation'), ref('agz_infra_suite.tag_workflow'), ref('agz_infra_suite.tag_database'), ref('agz_infra_suite.tag_ai')])]

record.field: services: tooljet: tty: true stdin_open: true image: tooljet/tooljet:ee-lts-latest restart: always env_file: .env expose: - 80 deploy: resources: limits: cpus: '1' memory: 2G depends_on: - postgres environment: SERVE_CLIENT: 'true' PORT: '80' command: npm run start:prod container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}' ports: - '{{ PORT_HTTP }}:80' networks: - internal - agz-proxy-network postgres: image: postgres:13 restart: always deploy: resources: limits: cpus: '2' memory: 3G volumes: - ../files/postgres:/var/lib/postgresql/data env_file: .env environment: - POSTGRES_USER=${PG_USER} - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=${PG_PASS} container_name: '{{ INSTANCE_CODE }}_postgres' networks: - internal networks: internal: driver: bridge agz-proxy-network: external: true

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Dump consistente de PostgreSQL (servicio en marcha) → $BACKUP_DATA_DIR docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_dump -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --no-owner -Fc' \ > "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump" echo "pg_dump OK ($(wc -c < "$BACKUP_DATA_DIR/db.dump") bytes)"

#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail cd "$SERVICE_PATH" # Levanta solo la DB, espera readiness y restaura docker compose up -d postgres for i in $(seq 1 30); do docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_isready -U "$POSTGRES_USER"' && break sleep 2 done docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c 'pg_restore -U "$POSTGRES_USER" -d "$POSTGRES_DB" --clean --if-exists --no-owner' \ < "$RESTORE_DATA_DIR/db.dump" # Verificación: la DB no quedó vacía (sh -c con dobles comillas; $VAR del contenedor con \$) N=$(docker compose exec -T postgres sh -c "psql -U \$POSTGRES_USER -d \$POSTGRES_DB -tAc \"SELECT count(*) FROM information_schema.tables WHERE table_schema='public'\"") echo "tablas restauradas: $N" test "$N" -gt 0
